title: Something that caught me off guard while building my EA.
author: Loose-Object-8913
selftext:
I’ve been trading gold manually for about 6 years and recently decided to turn my strategy into an automated system.

At the start I was obsessed with trying to improve the entries. I kept thinking if I could just find the “perfect signal” the results would improve.

Actually drove me insane.

But after running a lot of tests I realised something else mattered way more… how the system manages risk once it’s already in the trade.

Things like stop placement, volatility filters, and position sizing actually had a bigger impact on the equity curve than tweaking the entry logic.

It kind of flipped the way I was thinking about strategy design.

I recorded a short clip showing how the EA handles risk during trades.

Curious if anyone else who’s built systems ran into the same thing  ,did you find risk management mattered more than the signal itself?

MQL5- https://www.mql5.com/en/market/product/168039

YouTube- https://youtu.be/Q0jbBDaYQaY?si=Ti6VyZw\_lxqIkM2h

comments.author: Loose-Object-8913
comments.body:
Just to add a bit more context.

When I started coding the strategy I assumed improving entries would be the main challenge. Turns out the bigger challenge was controlling exposure and making sure the system behaves well in different market conditions.

Still learning a lot through testing but the process has been really interesting so far.

comments.author: dogazine4570
comments.body:
This is such a common (and painful) realization.

I went through something similar — spent months tweaking entry logic by tiny degrees, adding filters, stacking confirmations… and in backtests it would barely move the needle. Sometimes it even made things worse because the sample size shrank.

The big shift for me was understanding that entries mostly affect frequency and distribution, but risk management defines survival and equity curve shape.

A few things that made a bigger difference than entry tweaks:

- Stop placement relative to structure *and* volatility (ATR-based buffers helped avoid random noise stops).
- Position sizing that adapts to volatility instead of fixed lot sizing.
- Reducing risk after drawdown periods instead of trading full size through equity dips.
- Time-based exits for gold specifically — it can go dead for hours and then spike.

Gold especially punishes tight stops and oversized positions. Once I focused on controlling tail risk rather than predicting perfectly, my system became way more stable — even if win rate didn’t improve much.

Curious — did you find volatility-adjusted sizing or dynamic stops had the biggest impact?

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body: That’s almost exactly the path I went down. At first I spent way too much time trying to perfect entries — adding filters, tweaking indicators, small logic changes — but it didn’t really move the needle much. The biggest improvement came once I started focusing on volatility and risk instead. ATR-based stop distances and trailing logic made a big difference, especially on gold where tight stops get punished constantly. Another big challenge was getting the EA to recognize when the market is just ranging or going nowhere. Once I added volatility filters and dynamic stops it became much more stable. Still refining things through testing, but those changes definitely had the biggest impact for me.

comments.author: Automatic-Essay2175
comments.body: I’ve been doing this a long time. What you discovered is that your entry signal is bad.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body: Thanks for the feedback. How did you manage to get through it and filter out bad signals? I have several options but I chose to keep it simple for myself and for people.

replies.author: Automatic-Essay2175
replies.body: Years and years of rigorous backtesting

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body: Got a lot of fun to go through. Thank you again.

comments.author: bjxxjj
comments.body:
This resonates a lot. I went through a very similar phase when I first started automating a discretionary strategy — endless tweaking of entries, indicators, “one more filter,” etc. In hindsight, entries were the least fragile part of the system.

Risk management is where most EAs live or die. Stop logic, position sizing, and how the system behaves during volatility regime shifts matter far more than a slightly better signal. A mediocre entry with good risk control can survive; a great entry with poor risk logic will eventually blow up.

One thing that helped me was testing entry logic *in isolation* with fixed exits, then freezing it and only optimizing risk parameters afterward. Also worth testing how sensitive your system is to spread/slippage during high-volatility gold sessions — that surprised me more than anything.

Sounds like you’re on the right track.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body:
That’s a really good way of putting it 👍
Man, I went through exactly the same phase at the start. I was nonstop tweaking entries, stacking on more filters, changing indicators… convinced that if I just found a “better” signal everything would magically improve.
Spoiler: it barely moved the needle 😂
The real game-changer was when I finally shifted focus to the risk side — volatility filters, smarter stop placement, position sizing, and how the EA actually behaves once it’s in a trade. Especially on gold… that pair will eat tight stops for breakfast with all the noise.
Loved your tip about testing entry logic in isolation first, then freezing it and only tuning the risk parameters. I basically did the same thing and it made a huge difference.
Still learning every day, but that mindset flip honestly changed how I build everything now. Appreciate you sharing your experience, man — feels good knowing I’m not the only one who went down that rabbit hole!

comments.author: melbkiwi
comments.body: I learned the hard way that better entries don’t automatically build a better system. Most of the real improvement came from how the trade was managed after entry — stops, sizing, and knowing when market conditions had changed.

replies.author: Loose-Object-8913
replies.body: Once that’s sorted everything from tweaking to backtests make it a lot more fun and satisfying
